content.boundary: The equity and achievement effects of delaying tracking are strongly supported cross-nationally (Poland, Finland), but whether these gains hold in Hungary's specific context — given school choice, maintainer plurality, and the risk of sorting migrating to other channels — is only moderately evidenced and explicitly contested by the scenario's own uncertainty and equity fields.
content.change_lever: Cross-national difference-in-differences evidence associates earlier between-school tracking with greater inequality of outcomes without raising mean achievement, providing the core rationale for delaying selection.; Poland's 1999 reform (delaying selection from age 12 to 15) produced PISA gains of 0.25 SD math, 0.28 SD reading, 0.16 SD science, with at-risk students gaining approximately 1 SD, directly demonstrating the achievable effect-size range for structural delay.
content.name: Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)
content.system_problem: Raise the statutory age of first academic tracking to 14+ by phasing out 6- and 8-year gimnázium entry points into a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase, paired with explicit anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment.
